diagrams.title: Hyperledger Ecosystem Architecture
diagrams.description: A layered block diagram showing the complete Hyperledger ecosystem structure from foundation to applications
elements: Frameworks Layer (Hyperledger Fabric, Sawtooth, Indy, Iroha, Corda, Burrow)
Tools Layer (Caliper, Explorer, Composer, Cello, Quill)
Industry Applications Layer (Finance, Supply Chain, Healthcare, IoT)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the hierarchical structure of Hyperledger ecosystem and how different components relate to each other

diagrams.title: Hyperledger Project Classification
diagrams.description: A classification diagram showing all Hyperledger projects categorized by their functionality
elements: Distributed Ledger/Blockchain Frameworks (Fabric, Sawtooth, Iroha, Indy)
Tools (Caliper - benchmarking, Explorer - visualization, Composer - modeling, Cello - lifecycle management)
Support Libraries (Ursa - cryptography, Transact - transaction processing, Quilt - interoperability)
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to categorize and differentiate between various Hyperledger projects and their specific purposes

diagrams.title: Hyperledger Fabric Architecture
diagrams.description: A detailed block diagram of Hyperledger Fabric showing its modular architecture with peers, orderer, MSP, and channels
elements: Peer Nodes (Endorsing Peers, Committing Peers, Ordering Service)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the Fabric-specific architecture which is the most important Hyperledger framework for exams

diagrams.title: Hyperledger Transaction Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the complete transaction lifecycle in Hyperledger Fabric
elements: Step 4: Client sends transaction to Orderer
Step 5: Orderer packages transactions into blocks
Step 6: Orderer distributes blocks to committing peers
Step 7: Committing peers validate and commit to ledger
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the execute-order-validate architecture specific to Hyperledger Fabric

diagrams.title: Hyperledger vs Traditional Blockchains
diagrams.description: A comparison block diagram showing key architectural differences between Hyperledger and public blockchains
elements: Public Blockchain: Anonymous participants, consensus transparency, no permission, native cryptocurrency | Hyperledger: Identified participants, private consensus, permissioned, token-optional
Traditional: All nodes validate | Hyperledger: Selected endorsement nodes validate
Traditional: Open smart contracts | Hyperledger: Confidential chaincode execution
Traditional: Immutable transparency | Hyperledger: Selective visibility with channels
diagrams.purpose: Students understand why Hyperledger is preferred for enterprise use cases over public blockchains

charts.title: Hyperledger Frameworks Comparison
charts.content: Fabric: Modular architecture, pluggable consensus, channel-based privacy, use cases: supply chain, finance | Sawtooth: Modular design, PoET consensus, dynamic consensus, use cases: IoT, agriculture | Iroha: Mobile-friendly, role-based permissions, use cases: banking, identity | Indy: Decentralized identity, zero-knowledge proofs, use cases: identity management | Corda: Notary services, business logic focus, use cases: finance, insurance | Burrow: EVM-compatible, smart contract execution, use cases: dApps migration

charts.title: Hyperledger Project Selection Guide
charts.content: Decision guide for selecting appropriate Hyperledger project: Need identity management? → Indy/Aries | Need enterprise blockchain? → Fabric | Need IoT solutions? → Sawtooth | Need banking/finance? → Corda | Need mobile applications? → Iroha | Need smart contracts? → Burrow | Need benchmarking? → Caliper | Need visualization? → Explorer

charts.title: Hyperledger vs Other Blockchain Platforms
charts.content: | Feature | Hyperledger | Ethereum | Bitcoin | |---------|-------------|----------|---------| | Type | Permissioned | Public/Permissioned | Public | | Consensus | Pluggable | PoW/PoS | PoW | | Smart Contracts | Chaincode | Solidity | Limited | | Privacy | Channels/Private | Limited | None | | Performance | High (1000+ TPS) | Low (15-30 TPS) | Low (7 TPS) | | Identity | Managed (MSP) | Pseudo-anonymous | Pseudo-anonymous | | Use Case | Enterprise | DApps, DeFi | Currency |

charts.title: Hyperledger Version History and Key Releases
charts.content: Hyperledger Fabric: v0.6 (2016), v1.0 (2017), v1.1 (2018), v1.2 (2018), v1.3 (2019), v1.4 LTS (2019), v2.0 (2020), v2.2 LTS (2020), v2.5 (2022) | Hyperledger Sawtooth: v0.8 (2017), v1.0 (2018), v1.1 (2019), v2.0 (2021) | Hyperledger Iroha: v1.0 (2018), v2.0 (2021) | Hyperledger Indy: v1.0 (2017), v5.0 (2022) | Key Milestones: 2015 - Founded, 2016 - Fabric 0.6, 2017 - 30+ members, 2019 - Fabric 1.4 LTS, 2020 - Fabric 2.0 with privacy

branches: Frameworks (Fabric, Sawtooth, Iroha, Indy, Corda, Burrow)
Key Features (Permissioned, Modular, Interoperable, Scalable, Secure)
Use Cases (Finance, Supply Chain, Healthcare, Identity, IoT)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of Hyperledger for quick revision and concept retention
